burial.plot_notes: Mount Auburn Cemetery, Cambridge Middlesex County MA. FG cemetery #91241.

notable.notable_blurb: Universalist who disestablished Massachusetts state religion, 1834

career.title: Universalist minister
career.notes: Influential leader of the Universalist Church of America. Held the view of ultra-universalism (no afterlife punishment) along with Hosea Ballou.

career.title: Founder/Editor
career.notes: Founded and edited The Trumpet and Universalist magazine in 1828, succeeding Hosea Ballou's Universalist magazine.

career.title: Massachusetts State Legislator
career.notes: Cambridge representative in the Massachusetts legislature, 1831-1836. Chaired the committee that oversaw the disestablishment of the Congregational and Unitarian Churches from their privileged position in the Massachusetts Constitution. Held that 'no civil government has a right to compel the citizens to support any system of religion whatsoever.'

career.title: Co-founder
career.notes: Co-founded the Universalist Historical Society in 1834 with Thomas J. Sawyer of New York.

career.title: Author
career.notes: Authored numerous works including 'The Early Days of Thomas Whittemore' (1858, autobiography). Biography 'Memoir of Thomas Whittemore' by John Greenleaf Adams.

cross_connections.display_label: His daughter's father-in-law — Connecticut Congregationalist of eighty years.

narrative_blocks.body: From 1831 to 1836, Whittemore represented Cambridge in the Massachusetts legislature, chairing the committee that oversaw the disestablishment of the Congregational and Unitarian Churches from their privileged position in the state constitution. He held that 'no civil government has a right to compel the citizens to support any system of religion whatsoever,' and supported the 1834 popular referendum that brought Massachusetts into accord with the First Amendment.

Whittemore founded The Trumpet and Universalist magazine in 1828, succeeding Hosea Ballou's earlier Universalist magazine. With Thomas J. Sawyer of New York, he co-founded the Universalist Historical Society in 1834.

sources.short: Unitarian Universalist Historical Society biographical sketch
Whittemore, Thomas. The Early Days of Thomas Whittemore (autobiography, 1858)
Adams, John Greenleaf. Memoir of Thomas Whittemore

burial.plot_notes: Mount Auburn Cemetery, Cambridge Middlesex County MA. FG cemetery #91241. Same cemetery as her father Thomas Whittemore (X02773). 2 known Hooker connections (Lovice + via the Whittemore father easter egg) — below 3-Hooker-connection cemetery threshold.

parents.mother_research_notes: Lovice Corbett Whittemore Sr. (Nov 21, 1801 Milford Worcester County MA — Nov 2, 1882 Cambridge Middlesex MA, age 80). Wife of Thomas Whittemore. Daughter Lovice Jr. (this entry I00388) was named after her. Not built per Jalapeño rule (parent of in-law).

naming_inspiration.description: Named for her mother Lovice Corbett Whittemore Sr. (1801-1882).

sources.short: Find a Grave — Lovice Corbett Whittemore Cowles, Mount Auburn Cemetery

notable.primary_url_label: Wilford Woodruff Papers — John Egbert Cowles

career.title: Traveling salesman
career.notes: Traveling salesman for a clothing store in New York City. Living in Farmington 1893 per Cowles book #1687.

naming_inspiration.description: Named for his father Egbert Cowles (H01138). Middle name 'Egbert' is the patronymic; 'John' is the personal name.

narrative_blocks.header: Met Wilford Woodruff in Salt Lake City, 1870
narrative_blocks.body: On January 24-25, 1870, John Egbert Cowles called on LDS apostle Wilford Woodruff at his Salt Lake City office. Woodruff — born in Farmington 1807 and away from Connecticut for over thirty years — wrote in his journal that John Egbert was 'the first man I have met with from my Native town for the last 20 years.' They spent hours discussing Farmington families, with John Egbert reporting that 'Nearly all of the families of the Cowles & Demings were dead & their property gone into other Hands.'

narrative_blocks.header: Distant cousin of the Mormon apostle
narrative_blocks.body: His mother Sophia Sarah Woodruff descended from Matthew Woodruff — the 1640 Farmington Connecticut proprietor — through five 'Samuel' generations. Wilford Woodruff likewise descended from Matthew via his grandfather Eldad and father Aphek. Likely the unspoken reason the apostle sought him out when he learned Cowles was visiting Salt Lake.

narrative_blocks.header: Founding families gone, property in other hands
narrative_blocks.body: Per Wilford Woodruff's journal of January 25, 1870: 'Nearly all of the families of the Cowles and Demings were dead, and their property gone into other hands.' Two centuries after Farmington's founding, two of its founding-era families had been substantially displaced — the news delivered to Woodruff by a fellow former Farmington resident across a Salt Lake desk.

narrative_blocks.header: Traveling salesman for a New York clothing store
narrative_blocks.body: Per the 1873 Cowles family genealogy entry #1687, he worked as 'a traveling salesman for a clothing store in N.Y. City; living in Farmington, 1893.' He kept his Connecticut residence through his commercial travels, dying in Farmington at eighty in 1905.

sources.short: Wilford Woodruff Papers — John Egbert Cowles entry
Cowles Families in America #1687 — John Egbert⁷ Cowles

documents.document_blurb: Wilford Woodruff Journal entries January 24-25, 1870 — meetings with John Egbert Cowles in Salt Lake City.

INST002.name: Harvard College
INST002.notes: Rev. Thomas Shepard (I00002) was instrumental in founding Harvard and placing it at Cambridge. Rev. Samuel Shepard (H00008) graduated 1658; Samuel Shepard grandson (H00038) graduated 1685; Col. Roger Newton (H00044) also a graduate.
